one_line_takeaway: EXPGUARD shows that augmenting guardrail training data with Wikipedia-mined, domain-specific harmful/benign examples (finance, medical, legal) substantially improves detection of jargon-obscured harmful content (up to +15.3 F1 over WildGuard) without sacrificing general-benchmark performance, while an ablation cleanly attributes domain-specific gains to the domain-specific data slice and general-benchmark gains to the human-written/in-the-wild data slice.
key_finding: On EXPGUARDTEST, EXPGUARD scores 93.3% overall prompt-classification F1 (Financial 94.1%, Medical 91.2%, Legal 94.6%) versus WildGuard's 84.4% (an 8.9-point gain), and 92.7% overall response-classification F1 (Financial 96.7%, Medical 86.2%, Legal 92.4%) versus WildGuard's 77.4% (a 15.3-point gain). API-based guardrails (Detoxify, Perspective, OpenAI Moderation) score near zero (0.3-0.6%) on EXPGUARDTEST for both tasks. On the 8 public benchmarks (Table 3), EXPGUARD is roughly on par with WildGuard: 85.7% vs 84.2% average prompt F1, and 78.5% vs 78.8% average response F1. The ablation (Table 4) shows removing domain-specific training data drops EXPGUARDTEST prompt F1 from 93.3% to 85.3% (8.0-point drop), while removing human-written data drops public-benchmark response F1 from 78.5% to 73.9%, isolating which data component drives which capability. In jailbreak analysis, some baselines (Llama-Guard3, Aegis-Guard) paradoxically show increased detection rates after jailbreak transformations (e.g. CipherChat) are applied — the paper attributes this to baselines detecting jailbreak 'structural artifacts' rather than the underlying semantic harm; EXPGUARD+ (retrained with 270 AutoDAN-Turbo domain-specific adversarial examples) outperforms all baselines across both standard and domain-specific jailbreak settings.
core_contribution: The paper introduces EXPGUARD, a 7B-parameter LLM-based guardrail model specialized for content moderation in finance, healthcare, and law, plus EXPGUARDMIX, a 58,928-sample dataset (EXPGUARDTRAIN: 56,653 samples; EXPGUARDTEST: 2,275 expert-annotated samples spanning 964 financial, 771 medical, and 540 legal items) built via a three-stage pipeline: (1) domain terminology mining from Wikipedia filtered through Wikidata, GPT-4o, and human annotators (yielding 2,646 curated terms: finance 989, healthcare 1,012, law 645); (2) prompt/response generation using GPT-4o (harmful/benign prompts), Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 (compliant/harmful responses), and Gemma-3-27B-IT (refusals); and (3) category labeling via an ensemble of three proprietary LLMs (Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Qwen2.5-Max) with majority-vote consensus on a 13-category harm taxonomy plus deduplication via Sentence-BERT (cosine similarity > 0.9 removed). EXPGUARD is trained multi-task to predict binary safe/unsafe labels for prompts and, when present, responses.
threat_model: The paper's threat model is domain-specific adversarial/harmful content that evades general-purpose guardrails because it is phrased using technical jargon (e.g., a request to 'obscure high haircuts in asset evaluations' exploits domain-specific meaning of financial terms) rather than overtly harmful language. This is a content-moderation evasion threat, not a model-jailbreak-via-optimization threat per se, though Section 4.4 separately evaluates robustness against four established jailbreak attack methods (CipherChat, AutoDAN-Turbo, FlipAttack, GASP) applied to both standard (400 HarmBench-derived) and domain-specific (480 EXPGUARDTEST-derived) prompts, using Gemma-1.1-7B-IT as jailbreak generator and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as victim model.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 5): validated performance is confined to finance, medical, and legal domains only, with extension to other specialized fields unverified (though the pipeline is claimed to be adaptable); EXPGUARDMIX is English-only, with multilingual adaptation left as future work; despite RAG-grounding in Wikipedia and inclusion of in-the-wild data, the synthesized portion of the dataset may not capture the full diversity/unpredictability of live user queries, so generalization to dynamic real-world traffic is unverified; harmful content and adversarial tactics evolve over time, requiring periodic dataset updates. Additional grounding caveats not flagged by the authors as limitations but visible in the methodology: harmful-prompt generation required bypassing GPT-4o's own safety refusals via an affirmative-prefix jailbreak technique adopted from prior work (Lee et al., 2025), meaning the dataset's harmful examples are themselves the product of successfully jailbreaking a commercial model; and domain-expert verification of EXPGUARDTEST labels (Cohen's Kappa 0.89 prompt / 0.98 response) was performed only for the financial subset, with medical and legal subset reliability inferred by extension rather than independently verified.

datasets_used: EXPGUARDMIX (58,928 samples total; introduced by this paper)
EXPGUARDTRAIN (56,653 samples: 29,750 standalone prompts + 26,903 prompt-response pairs; 19,907 domain-specific + 36,746 general-domain)
EXPGUARDTEST (2,275 expert-annotated samples: 964 financial, 771 medical, 540 legal)
ToxicChat, OpenAI Moderation, XSTest, HarmBench, AegisSafetyTest2, WildGuardTest, BeaverTails, SafeRLHF (8 public evaluation benchmarks)
quantitative_results: EXPGUARDTEST overall prompt F1: EXPGUARD 93.3% vs WildGuard 84.4% (+8.9)
EXPGUARDTEST overall response F1: EXPGUARD 92.7% vs WildGuard 77.4% (+15.3)
EXPGUARDTEST prompt F1 by domain: Financial 94.1%, Medical 91.2%, Legal 94.6%
EXPGUARDTEST response F1 by domain: Financial 96.7%, Medical 86.2%, Legal 92.4%
8 public benchmarks avg prompt F1: EXPGUARD 85.7% vs WildGuard 84.2% (+1.5)
8 public benchmarks avg response F1: EXPGUARD 78.5% vs WildGuard 78.8% (WildGuard slightly ahead) and MD-Judge 78.2%
API-based guardrails (Detoxify, Perspective, OpenAI Moderation) on EXPGUARDTEST: 0.3-0.6% F1 (near zero)
Ablation: removing domain-specific data drops EXPGUARDTEST prompt F1 from 93.3% to 85.3% (-8.0)
Ablation: removing human-written data drops public-benchmark response F1 from 78.5% to 73.9%
Ablation: removing in-the-wild data drops public prompt F1 from 85.7% to 84.1%, EXPGUARDTEST prompt F1 93.3% to 93.2%
Terminology mining yielded 2,646 curated terms: finance 989, healthcare 1,012, law 645
Consensus/category-labeling filtering excluded 4.8% of generated domain-specific samples
Domain-expert vs LLM-ensemble label agreement (financial subset only): Cohen's Kappa 0.89 (prompts), 0.98 (responses)
EXPGUARD+ retrained with 270 AutoDAN-Turbo domain-specific jailbreak examples (matched 1:1 to 270 in-the-wild jailbreak prompts in EXPGUARDMIX)
Jailbreak evaluation set: 400 standard prompts (from HarmBench) + 480 domain-specific prompts (from EXPGUARDTEST)
